sanskrit_devanagari:
ज्ञानं तेऽहं सविज्ञानमिदं वक्ष्याम्यशेषतः ।
यज्ज्ञात्वा नेह भूयोऽन्यज्ज्ञातव्यमवशिष्यते ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
jñānaṁ te 'haṁ sa-vijñānam idaṁ vakṣyāmy aśeṣataḥ |
yaj jñātvā neha bhūyo 'nyaj jñātavyam avaśiṣyate || 2 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (transliteration body identical; punctuation rendering differs).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.002 = MBh_06,029.002). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: I shall now declare unto you in full this knowledge, both phenomenal and numinous. This being known, nothing further shall remain for you to know.
I shall now reveal unto you fully this knowledge and wisdom, knowing which nothing else remains to be known in this world.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Complete knowledge includes knowledge of the phenomenal world, the spirit behind it, and the source of both of them. This is transcendental knowledge. The Lord wants to explain the above-mentioned system of knowledge because Arjuna is Kṛṣṇa's confidential devotee and friend.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.2 Aham, I; vakṣyāmi, shall tell; te, you; aśeṣataḥ, in detail, fully; of that Knowledge about Myself, which is idam, this; jñānam, Knowledge; which is savijñānam, combined with realization, associated with personal enlightenment.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the promise of jñāna combined with vijñāna — book-knowledge fused with personal enlightenment. The 'nothing further to know' is the standard Upanishadic claim that brahma-jñāna is by-knowing-which-all-is-known.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.2 I will declare to you in full this knowledge having Me for its object, along with Vijnana or distinguishing knowledge. Vijnana is that knowledge of God in which His nature is distinguished from all things.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja distinguishes jñāna (knowledge of God) from vijñāna (the discriminative knowledge that distinguishes God's nature from all other things, animate and inanimate). The verse promises both — the substance and the discrimination by which it is distinguished from everything else.

positions.rendering: this knowledge, both phenomenal and numinous
Knowledge combined with realization, associated with personal enlightenment
knowledge of God along with Vijnana — distinguishing knowledge that separates God from all else

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators agree jñāna and vijñāna form a pair, but they differ on what vijñāna adds. Shankara reads vijñāna as personal-realization (as opposed to mere book-knowledge) — the Advaita pattern of śravaṇa/manana/nididhyāsana culminating in anubhava. Ramanuja reads vijñāna as discriminative knowledge — the capacity to distinguish God's nature from all other things, a doctrinal distinction Vishishtadvaita preserves. Prabhupada and Mukundananda flatten the pair into 'phenomenal/numinous' and 'knowledge/wisdom' respectively, both readings defensible but losing the Shankara-vs-Ramanuja distinction.

literal_meaning: I shall declare to you in full this knowledge with realization, knowing which nothing further remains here to be known.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names what he is about to give: jñāna combined with vijñāna — knowledge fused with realization (Shankara) or knowledge with discriminative understanding (Ramanuja). The verse makes the strong Upanishadic claim that what follows is by-knowing-which-all-is-known — the knowledge that exhausts further inquiry. The chapter that opens here promises a comprehensive disclosure, not a partial teaching.
